what_it_does: Implements the memory-curation UI and overlay for Vi: constants for memory kinds and scopes, helper lookups, a relative-time formatter, the MemoryAspect tabbed list of active learnings and raw feedback, MemoryCard/FeedbackCard/MemEmpty components, the ViMemory overlay that hosts the full identity mirror app or falls back to the memory aspect, and a launcher button. It also attaches components and helpers to window for composition by an identity browser.
observations: Defines MEM_KINDS and MEM_SCOPES lookup tables with icons, labels, and descriptions.
MemoryCard supports inline editing, kind/scope selection, active/inactive toggle, applied-this-session indicator, and forget.
FeedbackCard lets a raw feedback entry be promoted to an active memory or deleted.
ViMemory delegates to window.ViIdentity when present, otherwise renders MemoryAspect.
Many dependencies are globals: window.useFeedbackStore, window.MS_FEEDBACK, window.ViIdentity, Icon, Btn, ViMark.
Components and helpers are explicitly attached to window for external composition.
Escape key and overlay backdrop click close the overlay.
React hooks are aliased locally as useStateMem, useEffectMem, and useRefMem.
standouts: Memory is designed as a composable aspect of a larger identity mirror app rather than a standalone panel.
Each memory shows applied-this-session proof plus appliedCount and lastAppliedAt metrics.
Raw feedback can be promoted into durable memory directly from the log.
conventions: Uses window-polluted globals for store, feedback API, and child components instead of module imports.
Defines JSX components as functions and then assigns them to window.
Keeps constants and meta helpers at the top of the file.
Overlay backdrop close checks classList on the click target.
Aliases React hooks to local names.
concerns: Relies on undeclared globals with no local fallbacks, so the file is not self-contained.
timeAgo uses Date.now() during render, so relative labels only update on re-render.
scopeFilter initial handling duplicates the 'all' case and could be simplified.
No prop-types, TypeScript types, or runtime validation of global APIs.
notes: This is the memory-curation surface and overlay shell for Vi, built to be reused as one aspect inside a larger identity browser.
questions: Where are window.useFeedbackStore, window.MS_FEEDBACK, and window.ViIdentity defined and what is their exact contract?
What props do Icon, Btn, and ViMark expect?
How is the identity browser's aspect composition orchestrated via initialAspect, tweaks, and setTweak?
What persistence layer backs the feedback store?
purpose_vs_actual: Matches: the header says it owns the memory-curation UI and hosts the identity mirror-app, and the code implements both.
apparent_themes: Transparency through applied-this-session proof and counters
Composable UI aspects instead of monolithic settings pages
Promotion from ephemeral feedback to durable guidance
intention_signals: Make Vi's learned preferences inspectable, editable, and forgettable by the user, provide a bridge from raw session feedback into long-term memory, and allow the memory surface to be embedded inside a broader identity mirror application.
novelty: Treats memory not as a standalone settings panel but as one composable aspect of a larger identity mirror, exporting its cards and helpers on window so the identity browser can assemble them without reimplementation.
contribution: A concrete, reusable memory-curation UI with kind/scope taxonomy, inline editing, active/inactive toggling, applied-this-session proof, feedback-to-memory promotion, and an overlay shell that can either host the full identity app or render the memory aspect alone.
summary_for_embedding: feedback-memory.jsx is a React module that implements Vi's memory-curation UI—memory and feedback-log lists with inline editing, scoping, activation toggles, applied-session proof, and promote-to-memory actions—plus an overlay wrapper and launcher button, exposing components on window for composition by an identity browser.
intention_for_embedding: It was intended to give users a transparent, editable, persistent view of Vi's learned guidance and a path to turn session feedback into durable memory, while serving as a composable aspect within a larger identity mirror app.

symbols.does: Returns the metadata object for a memory kind, defaulting to the custom/note kind.
symbols.description: Looks up the given kind id in MEM_KINDS and returns its label, icon, and description; falls back to MEM_KINDS[6] (custom) for unknown kinds.

symbols.does: Returns the human-readable label for a memory scope id.
symbols.description: Looks up the scope id in MEM_SCOPES and returns its label, or returns the raw id if not found.

symbols.does: Formats a timestamp as a relative age string.
symbols.description: Computes elapsed seconds from the given timestamp to Date.now() and returns 'just now', minutes/hours/days ago, or 'never' for missing input.

symbols.does: Closes the overlay when the Escape key is pressed.
symbols.description: Event handler defined inside ViMemory's effect that calls onClose if the key is 'Escape'.

symbols.does: Renders a single learning/memory with editing, kind/scope controls, toggle, and forget.
symbols.description: Displays a memory's kind badge, scope selector, active toggle, applied-this-session indicator, editable text, kind selector, and metadata; supports saving, canceling, and forgetting the learning via window.MS_FEEDBACK.

symbols.does: Persists edited memory text and exits editing mode.
symbols.description: Calls window.MS_FEEDBACK.updateLearning with the trimmed text (falling back to the original if empty) and sets editing to false.

symbols.does: Renders a raw feedback entry with promote-to-memory and delete actions.
symbols.description: Shows the feedback action type, target, timestamp, content, and either a 'Make this a memory' button or a promoted badge, plus a delete button via window.MS_FEEDBACK.

symbols.does: Renders an empty-state message for memory or feedback lists.
symbols.description: Displays a Vi badge, title, and explanatory subtitle tailored to whether the list is truly empty or merely filtered.
